result.chapter_summary: Hirsch argues that the fragmentation of the American school curriculum and the decline in literacy are primarily caused by the dominance of 'romantic formalism' in educational theory. He contends that schools fail to bridge the achievement gap because they treat literacy as a set of content-neutral skills rather than a knowledge-dependent process that requires early systematic instruction in cultural information.

claims.text: School is the only institution for acculturating children that is susceptible to public policy control.
claims.source_passage: Family, church, and other institutions play an important role, but school is the only institution that is susceptible to public policy control.

claims.text: The assigned role of modern schools is to prepare children for broader social activities and train them in literate public culture.
claims.source_passage: In the modern age, the role assigned to our schools is to prepare our children for the broader activities of society and to train them in the literate public culture.

claims.text: American schools have failed to perform their role effectively because they have followed faulty educational ideas.
claims.source_passage: Our schools have played this role less well than they should, chiefly because they have followed faulty educational ideas.

claims.text: The decline of American literacy and the fragmentation of the school curriculum were caused by the dominance of romantic formalism in educational theory.
claims.source_passage: The decline of American literacy and the fragmentation of the American school curriculum have been chiefly caused by the ever growing dominance of romantic formalism in educational theory during the past half century.

claims.text: Educational theorists and policymakers deserve more blame for educational shortcomings than social changes or teacher incompetence.
claims.source_passage: But the chief blame should fall on faulty theories promulgated in our schools of education and accepted by educational policymakers.

claims.text: Prior knowledge is the prerequisite for learning new information.
claims.source_passage: The more you know, the more you can learn.

claims.text: Identifying and imparting missing information to children is most important and manageable in the earliest grades.
claims.source_passage: identifying and imparting the information a child is missing is most important in the earliest grades, when the task is most manageable.

claims.text: Technical reading skills of disadvantaged children at age six are equal to those of children from literate families.
claims.source_passage: As Dr. Jeanne Chall has pointed out, the technical reading skills of disadvantaged children at age six are still on a par with those of children from literate families.

claims.text: The divergence in reading skills between socioeconomic groups after age six is primarily caused by a lack of elementary cultural knowledge in low-income pupils.
claims.source_passage: Yet only a year or so later, their reading skills begin to diverge according to socioeconomic status, chiefly because low-income pupils lack elementary cultural knowledge.

claims.text: Supplying missing knowledge early enhances a child's motivation and intellectual self-confidence.
claims.source_passage: Supplying missing knowledge to children early is of tremendous importance for enhancing their motivation and intellectual self-confidence

claims.text: Schools will not systematically impart background information as long as they believe specific information is irrelevant to language arts skills.
claims.source_passage: Yet schools will never systematically impart missing background information as long as they continue to accept the formalistic principle that specific information is irrelevant to 'language arts skills.'

claims.text: Educational formalism incorrectly conceives of literacy as a set of content-neutral techniques comparable to physical skills like skating.
claims.source_passage: Educational formalism holds that reading and writing are like baseball and skating; formalism conceives of literacy as a set of techniques that can be developed by proper coaching and practice.

claims.text: The 'skill' conception of reading becomes an oversimplification once students move beyond decoding to reading for meaning.
claims.source_passage: The skill idea becomes an oversimplification as soon as students start reading for meaning rather than for cracking the alphabetic code.

claims.text: Reading for meaning is not a uniform skill but varies depending on the specific content of the writing.
claims.source_passage: The trouble is that reading for meaning is a different sort of game entirely. It is different every time, depending on what the piece of writing is about.

claims.text: Possessing the information taken for granted by a text is the decisive skill of reading.
claims.source_passage: Knowing such information is the decisive skill of reading.

claims.text: Reading for meaning is fundamentally different from simply decoding the alphabetic code or learning text strategies.
claims.source_passage: The trouble is that reading for meaning is a different sort of game entirely. It is different every time, depending on what the piece of writing is about.

claims.text: Every text, including elementary ones, implies information that it takes for granted and does not explain.
claims.source_passage: Every text, even the most elementary, implies information that it takes for granted and doesn't explain.

claims.text: Educational formalism incorrectly assumes that the specific content used in language arts instruction does not matter as long as it connects to a child's existing knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Educational formalism assumes that the specific contents used to teach "language arts" do not matter so long as they are closely tied to what the child already knows

claims.text: Current schoolbooks in language arts fail to systematically convey a body of culturally significant information across grade levels.
claims.source_passage: Current schoolbooks in language arts pay little systematic attention to conveying a body of culturally significant information from grade to grade.

claims.text: Modern reading textbooks are screened for readability scores and abstract skill sequences rather than the cultural information they contain.
claims.source_passage: Texts now used to teach reading and writing are screened not for the information they convey but for their readability scores and their fit with the sequence of abstract skills that a child is expected to acquire.

claims.text: Learning to read is often erroneously represented as a neutral, technical process of skill acquisition.
claims.source_passage: those I have consulted represent learning to read as a neutral, technical process of skill acquisition

claims.text: Effective reading depends on acquiring factual and traditional schemata.
claims.source_passage: it has become clear that effective reading depends on acquiring factual and traditional schemata.

claims.text: Teachers are currently trained only to impart skills rather than to master specific academic disciplines or factual information.
claims.source_passage: teachers are not expected to have mastered particular factual and traditional information or any special academic discipline; they are trained only to impart skills.

claims.text: The failure to teach traditional literate culture causes a disintegration of cultural memory and a decline in national communication.
claims.source_passage: The inevitable effect of this fundamental educational mistake has been a gradual disintegration of cultural memory, causing a gradual decline in our ability to communicate.

claims.text: Illiteracy is a subcategory of the general inability to communicate.
claims.source_passage: The mistake has therefore been a chief cause of illiteracy, which is a subcategory of the inability to communicate.

claims.text: Educational theorists have mistakenly adopted a fatalistic view that schools cannot overcome the influence of a child's family background.
claims.source_passage: Professors of education have accepted the view that family background is so important and so deeply ingrained in young children that the schools are powerless to make a significant difference

claims.text: Differences in academic achievement are strongly correlated with students' family backgrounds.
claims.source_passage: differences in academic achievement were more strongly correlated with students' family backgrounds than with differences in the schools they attended.

claims.text: The 1966 Coleman report established a strong correlation between family background and academic achievement based on data from four thousand schools.
claims.source_passage: That the Coleman report should have shown a strong correlation between family background and academic achievement is not surprising.

claims.text: Children from literate households gain the literate culture necessary for reading more readily than those from illiterate households.
claims.source_passage: Common sense predicts that children from literate households can more readily gain the literate culture necessary for reading than those from illiterate households.

claims.text: Minority children who lack models of academic achievement suffer from academic defeatism, which intensifies the achievement gap.
claims.source_passage: Moreover, we have become increasingly sensitive to subtle psychological influences that intensify this effect, such as the academic defeatism of minority children who lack models of academic achievement.

claims.text: The lack of American background information is less damaging to children who possess traditions of academic achievement and literacy in another language.
claims.source_passage: Where low-income children do have traditions of academic achievement and literacy in another language, as do many Asian-Americans, the initial lack of American background information is less damaging.

claims.text: The current consensus among educators that schools cannot offset cultural deprivation is an unwarranted inference drawn from sociological data.
claims.source_passage: The flaw in the current view is not that the sociological observations are wrong (they have been reconfirmed many times), but that the inferences drawn from them are unwarranted.

claims.text: A significant part of children's education in the United States currently occurs outside the school system rather than inside it.
claims.source_passage: We might infer from them that the schools have not done much to compensate for cultural deprivations; that the significant part of our children's education has been going on outside rather than inside the schools

claims.text: The conclusion that schools are ineffectual has historically undermined the argument for specific curricula, requirements, or educational policies.
claims.source_passage: If schools were so ineffectual in affecting the educational outcomes of their students, it becomes difficult to argue on behalf of any given curriculum, requirement, or policy.

claims.text: Educational commentators have wrongly assumed that a fragmented school curriculum is a permanent and necessary feature of the educational system.
claims.source_passage: It has been silently assumed that our recently fragmented school curriculum is a permanent and necessary feature of our educational system.

claims.text: Literacy levels have declined even among children from literate homes as the school curriculum has become more incoherent.
claims.source_passage: Historically, as the school curriculum has become more incoherent, literacy has declined even among children from literate homes.

claims.text: Family background is not the uniquely decisive determinant of a child's level of literacy; specific curricula also significantly affect outcomes.
claims.source_passage: This fact has an important corollary: family background is not the uniquely decisive determinant of our children's level of literacy. Specific curricula also significantly affect academic outcomes.

claims.text: The primary factor in academic achievement is the amount of time a student spends studying the specific material to be mastered.
claims.source_passage: it appears that the chief factor in academic achievement is the time the student spends in studying the material to be mastered.

claims.text: Private schools achieve better academic results for all students, including those from low-income families, compared to public schools.
claims.source_passage: it finds that private schools produce better academic results for all students, even when family backgrounds are taken into account. Children from low-income families reach higher levels of achievement in private schools than they do in public schools.

claims.text: Private schools are more effective because their curricula impart more literate information and focus more on academic courses than public schools.
claims.source_passage: But in light of what we know about literacy, an important factor must be that curricula of private schools impart more literate information than those of public schools.

claims.text: Private schools offer fewer nontraditional and vocational courses compared to public schools.
claims.source_passage: Private schools offer fewer nontraditional and vocational courses and give each student proportionally more academic courses.

claims.text: Private schools produce better academic results for all students than public schools, even when family backgrounds are controlled for.
claims.source_passage: private schools produce better academic results for all students, even when family backgrounds are taken into account.

claims.text: Children from low-income families achieve higher levels of academic success in private schools than in public schools.
claims.source_passage: Children from low-income families reach higher levels of achievement in private schools than they do in public schools.

claims.source_passage: Private schools offer fewer nontraditional and vocational courses and give each student proportionally more academic courses.

claims.text: The crucial factor in student achievement is 'time on task' spent in content courses.
claims.source_passage: the crucial factor is 'time on task.' In private schools, both middle-class and disadvantaged students spend more time in content courses and are exposed to more of the information that belongs to literate culture.

claims.text: Higher literacy levels are achievable for many more students if they are provided with a content-rich curriculum early in their education.
claims.source_passage: The implication is that many more students could become highly literate if they were presented with the right sort of curriculum, particularly in their early years.

claims.text: The American school curriculum is fragmented both horizontally across subjects and vertically within subjects.
claims.source_passage: The American school curriculum is fragmented both horizontally across subjects and vertically within subjects.

claims.text: The history of American secondary education since the 1920s is characterized by the dominance of the 'Cardinal Principles' over the humanistic 'Committee of Ten' principles.
claims.source_passage: Between the 1920s and the 1970s, the history of American secondary education has been one of the increasing dominance of the Cardinal Principles and the corresponding decline of the principles of the Committee of Ten

claims.text: The 1893 Report of the Committee of Ten held a modern, integrated conception of academic subjects.
claims.source_passage: the 1893 report was surprisingly modern in its conception of the various subjects.

claims.text: English composition should not be treated as a skill isolated from specific subject matter.
claims.source_passage: It was emphatic, for instance, in holding that English composition should not be conceived as a skill in isolation from subject matter

claims.text: Educational formalism—the idea of testing writing ability without regard to knowledge of the subject—is a theoretically and practically flawed approach.
claims.source_passage: It believes that there are serious theoretical and practical objections to estimating a student's power to write a language on the basis of a theme composed not for the sake of expounding something that he knows or thinks, but merely for the sake of showing his ability to write.

claims.text: The Committee of Ten (1893) utilized educational formalism to defend the traditional curriculum, arguing that specific subjects like Latin, Greek, and geography 'train the mind.'
claims.source_passage: It subscribed to the claim that studying Latin and Greek "trains the mind" and that studying geography enhances "(1) the power of observation, (2) the powers of scientific imagination, and (3) the powers of reasoning."

claims.text: James H. Baker argued that the choice of subjects in schools is not a matter of indifference because cognitive power is derived from specific knowledge rather than abstract training.
claims.source_passage: I cannot endorse expressions that appear to sanction the idea that the choice of subjects in secondary schools may be a matter of comparative indifference. . . . Power comes through knowledge; we can not conceive of observation and memory in the abstract.

claims.text: The 1918 report 'Cardinal Principles of Secondary Education' explicitly rejected the earlier focus on subject matter in favor of social adjustment aims.
claims.source_passage: In deliberate contrast to the 1893 report, Cardinal Principles of Secondary Education explicitly rejected the earlier focus on subject matter. Instead, it stressed the seven fundamental aims of education in a democracy

claims.text: The ideological shift in American education after 1918 was intended to stress utility and the direct application of knowledge to produce productive and happy citizens.
claims.source_passage: Henceforth it should stress utility and the direct application of knowledge, with the goal of producing good, productive, and happy citizens.

claims.text: The conception of natural human growth has been the most decisive influence on American educational theory between 1927 and 1987.
claims.source_passage: From the perspective of intellectual history, the conception of natural human growth has been the most decisive influence on American educational theory over the past six decades.

claims.text: Modern schools of education present romantic naturalistic ideas under the guise of developmental psychology.
claims.source_passage: Although schools of education have clothed this romantic idea in the language of developmental psychology, their basic assumptions owe more to Rousseau than to Piaget.

claims.text: John Dewey was fundamentally a disciple of Jean-Jacques Rousseau regarding his basic educational assumptions.
claims.source_passage: In basic educational assumptions Dewey was a disciple of Rousseau.

claims.text: The contemporary educational doctrine that a child's positive self-concept is the key to learning is a direct descendant of Rousseau's principles in 'Emile'.
claims.source_passage: Even today, Rousseau's principles reappear in the doctrine, straight from Emile, that a child's positive self-concept is the true key to learning.

claims.text: John Dewey and his followers rejected traditional, literate culture as 'abstract' and 'rote-learned' material.
claims.source_passage: Dewey and his followers agreed further with Rousseau and Wordsworth in scorning secondhand, bookish education. Dewey attacked the abstract, rote-learned material of literate culture

claims.text: The 1918 report mandated that the value of school subjects be tested by their application to life activities rather than their internal logical organization.
claims.source_passage: Subject values must be tested in terms of the laws of learning and the application of knowledge to the activities of life, rather than primarily in terms of the demands of any subject as a logically organized science.

claims.text: Edward Thorndike's research provided the scientific justification used by reformers to discredit the traditional curriculum's claim of 'general mental discipline.'
claims.source_passage: By 1918 educational psychology, particularly the work of Edward Thorndike, had vindicated Baker's objection to the idea that skills in one area are automatically transferred to another. This finding was used to cast doubt on the entire rationale of the traditional curriculum.

claims.text: The 1918 Cardinal Principles report shifted the focus of American schools toward the needs of the child and society, as recommended by John Dewey.
claims.source_passage: The schools were henceforth to focus upon the needs of the child and society, as Dewey had recommended.

claims.text: Edward Thorndike's psychological research vindicated the claim that skills acquired in one subject area do not automatically transfer to another.
claims.source_passage: By 1918 educational psychology, particularly the work of Edward Thorndike, had vindicated Baker's objection to the idea that skills in one area are automatically transferred to another.

claims.text: The psychological finding regarding the lack of skill transfer was used to undermine the entire rationale of the traditional curriculum.
claims.source_passage: This finding was used to cast doubt on the entire rationale of the traditional curriculum.

claims.text: The 1918 report rejected the 1893 assumption that all students, regardless of their future career path, should follow the same course of study.
claims.source_passage: In 1918, modern psychology was also invoked to oppose the view expressed in 1893 that everyone, college bound or not, should take the same course of study.

claims.text: The emphasis on individual differences in the 1918 report required the adoption of educational formalism.
claims.source_passage: Because every child was to be brought to an acceptable level of reading, writing, and arithmetic... the emphasis on diversity required an acceptance of educational formalism.

claims.text: Educational formalism conceives of fundamental processes like reading and writing as purely formal skills that can be mastered using any variety of content.
claims.source_passage: For if all are to attain literacy and numeracy through different materials suited to their different temperaments, the 'fundamental processes' of reading, writing, and arithmetic must be conceived as purely formal skills.

claims.text: The 1918 Cardinal Principles report attempted to address the demographic pressures caused by mass immigration and a rapidly expanding school population.
claims.source_passage: The later report tried to adjust school aims to the new situation created by immigration and a consistent doubling of the school population every ten years.

claims.text: The 1918 report was more democratic than the 1893 report in its commitment to providing high school education for every American child.
claims.source_passage: Reflecting these demographic changes, the Cardinal Principles expressed a laudable commitment to educate every American through the high school level—an ideal that had been rejected as unfeasible by the Committee of Ten.

claims.text: The 1893 Committee of Ten report was more egalitarian than the 1918 report because it insisted on a uniform educational foundation for all students.
claims.source_passage: Yet the Committee of Ten had been highly democratic in stating that all students should receive exactly the same educational foundation as long as they could stay in school. On this score, the earlier document was more egalitarian than the later.

claims.text: The 1918 report implicitly accepted a permanent social and economic stratification by assuming some children could not master literate culture.
claims.source_passage: The stress on individual differences and on vocational training implicitly accepted a permanent stratification of economic and social positions.

claims.text: The 1893 ideal that all students should start from the same educational foundation is a democratic ideal that needs to be renewed.
claims.source_passage: Certainly the 1893 idea that everyone can and should start out from the same educational foundation was an admirable democratic ideal that needs to be renewed.

claims.text: The system of American education since 1918 has suffered from the premature application of psychological theories.
claims.source_passage: From our current perspective, one great failing of the Cardinal Principles was its premature conclusion about the proper application of what were then new psychological theories.

claims.text: The 1918 Cardinal Principles report implicitly accepted a permanent stratification of economic and social positions by stressing individual differences and vocational training.
claims.source_passage: The stress on individual differences and on vocational training implicitly accepted a permanent stratification of economic and social positions.

claims.text: American education since 1918 has been significantly based on Thorndike's claim that learning does not transfer from one area to another.
claims.source_passage: The system of American education since 1918 has continued to be partly based on Thorndike's claim that training is not transferred from one area of learning to another.

claims.text: The principle of the nontransfer of training is not as absolute as the 1918 report suggested.
claims.source_passage: The principle of nontransfer is not as absolute as the 1918 report suggested.

claims.text: The human mind operates by analogy and generalizes from individual cases, allowing for degrees of transfer.
claims.source_passage: The human mind operates by analogy in many of its activities, and it generalizes from individual cases.

claims.text: The 1918 report was intellectually inconsistent because it rejected the transfer of training for Latin while assuming it for reading and writing.
claims.source_passage: Thus, while repudiating the idea of the transfer of training for Latin, the 1918 report unwittingly adopted it for reading and writing.

claims.text: The 1918 report erroneously assumed that reading and writing are formal skills that can be applied to different tasks regardless of subject matter.
claims.source_passage: The report assumed that reading and writing are formal skills that can be applied to different tasks, regardless of subject matter.

claims.text: John Dewey's assumption that children would become highly literate under 'learning-by-doing' principles turned out to be incorrect.
claims.source_passage: Despite his opposition to book-centered instruction, Dewey assumed that children would become highly literate under his learning-by-doing principles of education. Although that assumption turned out to be incorrect...

claims.text: The institutionalization of 'tracking' and 'grouping' produced three academic castes receiving different kinds of information.
claims.source_passage: The principle of adjusting to diverse capacities produced, in effect, three academic castes, each of which received different kinds of information.

claims.text: In language arts, tracking led to content becoming an arbitrary vehicle, providing Shakespeare for some and sports stories for others.
claims.source_passage: In language arts courses, for instance, where content became an arbitrary vehicle to inculcate the "fundamental processes" of reading and writing, the tracking system led to Shakespeare for some, sports and fantasy stories for others.

claims.text: Tracking and vocational courses multiplied course offerings, leading ultimately to the extreme horizontal fragmentation of the 'shopping mall school.'
claims.source_passage: The introduction of such courses, like the introduction of the tracking system, further multiplied the different sorts of courses offered in school, leading ultimately to the shopping mall school with its extreme horizontal fragmentation.

claims.text: The principles of 1918 led to history being transformed into social studies because history was viewed as an inefficient means of achieving social goals.
claims.source_passage: History was seen as an indirect and inefficient means of achieving those social goals, and was transformed into social studies.

claims.text: The introduction of tracking in schools reinforced the curriculum fragmentation initially caused by vocational courses.
claims.source_passage: The fragmentation that was being introduced by tracking reinforced the fragmentation that was being introduced by vocational courses.

claims.text: The 1918 principles aimed to make schools directly useful for preparing children for citizenship, work, and leisure rather than academic mastery.
claims.source_passage: Schools were to be directly useful in preparing children for citizenship, work, and leisure.

claims.text: The transformation of history into social studies was driven by the view that history was an indirect and inefficient way to achieve social goals.
claims.source_passage: History was seen as an indirect and inefficient means of achieving those social goals, and was transformed into social studies.

claims.text: Social studies courses prioritize the 'activities of life' over the logical organization of a specific academic subject.
claims.source_passage: Like vocational courses, social studies courses were directed 'to the activities of life rather than to the demands of any subject as a logically organized science.'

claims.text: The shift from history to social studies led inexorably to a lack of shared historical information among students.
claims.source_passage: But since the applications of social studies 'to the activities of life' were infinitely various, different schools came to teach different sorts of information in them, and the transformation of history into social studies led inexorably to a lack of shared historical information.

claims.text: Graduates of private schools in the mid-20th century were generally more literate than public school graduates because private schools maintained traditional content.
claims.source_passage: The private schools were less influenced by the new theories, and many of them continued to provide traditional content. As a consequence, their graduates were, on the whole, more literate than those who came from the public schools.

claims.text: The disparity in literacy between public and private schools exacerbated the social class stratifications that earlier reformers had intended to avoid.
claims.source_passage: Such differences exacerbated the class stratifications that the educators of 1893 had sought to avoid.

claims.text: The 'Life Adjustment' movement of the 1940s and 50s was essentially an extension of the 1918 progressive educational principles.
claims.source_passage: The life adjustment movement of the 1940s and 1950s continued the advance of the Cardinal Principles. It was 'indistinguishable in everything but name' from many already established versions of progressive education, going back to 1918.

claims.text: Proponents of Life Adjustment education believed it was the best approach for all American youth, not just those considered disadvantaged.
claims.source_passage: Nor did the conference participants believe that life adjustment education should be directed only to disadvantaged students. It was regarded as a universal principle, 'best suited to meet the needs of all American youth.'

claims.text: Current educational practices that grant academic credit for non-academic life-skills courses are grounded in Life Adjustment theory.
claims.source_passage: This view is the ground for our current practice of giving academic credit for such courses as family relations, adolescent parenting, and apartment and income property management.

claims.text: The romantic formalism of the 1960s and 1970s was a logical and extreme extension of the educational theories dominant for the previous fifty years.
claims.source_passage: The romantic formalism of the sixties and seventies was an extreme yet logical extension of the romantic formalism that has been the dominant theory taught in our schools of education over the past fifty years.

claims.text: Open educators prioritize the individual quality and meaning of a child's experience over the acquisition of any specific body of knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Open educators worry less about whether a child has had a particular experience than about the quality and the meaning for him of the experiences he has had.

claims.text: The 1980s counterreform movement toward a traditional curriculum is a positive development reflecting the good sense of the American public.
claims.source_passage: The counterreform of the 1980s seems bent upon a return to a more traditional curriculum. This welcome course correction demonstrates the underlying good sense of the American people.

claims.text: The counterreform of the 1980s represents a welcome course correction by the American people toward a traditional curriculum.

claims.source_passage: School administrators and senior professors in schools of education who were trained in the theories of the Cardinal Principles naturally resist fundamental deviations from them

claims.text: Teaching traditional facts unimaginatively or as ends in themselves is an invalid and deplorable approach to education.

claims.text: Dry incompetence is not the inevitable alternative to lively ignorance in the classroom.
claims.source_passage: But dry incompetence is not the necessary alternative to lively ignorance.

claims.text: Higher values and accumulated wisdom are not the exclusive property of Western culture but are found in all civilized traditions.
claims.source_passage: Higher values comprising humankind's accumulated wisdom about human nature are found in all civilized traditions, East and West, ancient and modern, popular and aristocratic.

claims.text: Traditional education is the only method that yields the flexible skill of mature literacy.

claims.text: Narrow vocational education is made obsolete by changing technology and is unsuited for the modern world.
claims.source_passage: Narrow vocational education, adjusted to the needs of the moment, is made ever more obsolete by changing technology.

claims.text: The modern world requires education for change rather than for static job competencies.
claims.source_passage: What is required is education for change, not for static job competencies.

claims.text: Individuality can only be expressed by children in relation to the traditions of their society, which must be learned.
claims.source_passage: Children can express individuality only in relation to the traditions of their society, which they have to learn. The greatest human individuality is developed in response to a tradition, not in response to disorderly, uncertain, and fragmented education.

claims.text: Young Americans educated under individualistic theories are less literate and less able to express individuality than their predecessors.
claims.source_passage: Americans in their teens and twenties who were brought up under individualistic theories are not less conventional than their predecessors, only less literate, less able to express their individuality.

claims.text: Anti-traditional progressive goals actually depend upon the acquisition of traditional information.

claims.text: American education requires both commonality in goals and flexibility in means.
claims.source_passage: She argues that we need both commonality and flexibility in American education and that there is no reason we cannot have both at once.

claims.source_passage: One can think of the school curriculum as consisting of two complementary parts, which might be called the extensive curriculum and the intensive curriculum.

claims.text: The extensive curriculum should consist of traditional literate knowledge—the information, attitudes, and assumptions shared by literate Americans.
claims.source_passage: The content of the extensive curriculum is traditional literate knowledge, the information, attitudes, and assumptions that literate Americans share — cultural literacy.

claims.text: The world knowledge shared by literate adults is typically elementary and incomplete in nature.
claims.source_passage: The nature of this world knowledge as it exists in the minds of literate adults is typically elementary and incomplete.

claims.text: The information that literate people dependably share is extensive but limited.
claims.source_passage: In short, the information that literate people dependably share is extensive but limited — a characteristic central to this discussion.

claims.text: Possession of limited background information is a necessary preliminary to acquiring more detailed information.
claims.source_passage: Yet students' possession of that limited information is a necessary preliminary to their acquiring more detailed information.

claims.source_passage: This extensive network of associations constitutes the part of the curriculum that has to be known by every child and must be common to all the schools of the nation.

claims.text: The extensive curriculum is a necessary but insufficient basis for education.

claims.source_passage: The extensive curriculum can be taught in a highly formal traditional school or in a more informal progressive school.
