claims.source_passage: The intensive curriculum is the more pluralistic element of my proposal, because it ensures that individual students, teachers, and schools can work intensively with materials that are appropriate for their diverse temperaments and aims.

claims.text: A two-part curriculum structure avoids the requirement that all children study identical detailed materials.
claims.source_passage: The conception of a two-part curriculum avoids the idea that all children should study identical materials.

claims.text: Schools can impart extensive information alongside intensive knowledge without imposing an arbitrary core curriculum.
claims.source_passage: Schools  can  find  means of  imparting  extensive  information  side  by  side  with  an  approach that  conveys  intensive  knowledge  as  well,  without  imposing  an arbitrary  core  curriculum.

claims.text: The 'cheerful facts' possessed by the Major-General in 'Pirates of Penzance' were part of the standard intellectual baggage of every literate schoolboy in nineteenth-century Britain.
claims.source_passage: These  "cheerful  facts"  are  all  the  Major-General  knows,  and  they were  part  of  the  intellectual  baggage  possessed  by  every  literate schoolboy  in  nineteenth-century  Britain.

claims.text: To understand how isolated facts fit together, humans must acquire mental models or schemata of how they cohere.
claims.source_passage: To  understand  how  isolated  facts  fit  together in  some  coherent  way,  we  must  always  acquire  mental  models  of how  they  cohere

claims.text: Schemata for understanding factual coherence can only be gained from detailed, intensive study and experience.
claims.source_passage: these  schemata  can  come  only  from  detailed, intensive  study  and  experience.

claims.text: Almost any specific example within a category (e.g., any battle or any Shakespeare play) is sufficient to help a student gain a schematic conception of that category.
claims.source_passage: Almost  any  battle  will  do  to  gain  a  coherent  idea  of  battles.  Any Shakespeare  play  will  do  to  gain  a  schematic  conception  of  Shakespeare

claims.text: The choice of intensive curricular materials should depend on student and teacher interest, local community preferences, and school values.
claims.source_passage: Our  choice  of  intensive  curricular  ma- terials  can  vary  with  circumstances  and  should  depend  on  many grounds  for  choice,  including  student  and  teacher  interest,  local community  preferences,  and  the  aims  and  values  that  predominate in  particular  schools  or  classrooms.

claims.text: There is a limit to the flexibility of the intensive curriculum; specific canonical works cannot be replaced by modern popular works if the goal is to understand a specific tradition (e.g., Neil Simon cannot replace Shakespeare).
claims.source_passage: But  there  is  a  limit  to  the  flex- ibility  of  the  intensive  curriculum.  If  we  want  people  to  have  a conception  of  Shakespearean  drama,  then  a  play  by  Neil  Simon  is not  a  satisfactory  substitute  for  a  play  by  Shakespeare.

claims.text: The full range of cultural literacy is conveyed by a broad variety of experiences both inside and outside of school.
claims.source_passage: It  is  probably  true  that  the  full  range  of  cultural  literacy  will  be conveyed  by  a  broad  variety  of  experiences,  inside  and  outside school.

claims.text: Schools can play a central role in conveying shared information by indexing cultural literacy for use by curriculum developers.
claims.source_passage: But  school  can  play  a  central  role  in  conveying  shared  in- formation.  First  of  all,  the  nature  of  that  information  can  be  de- termined,  and  it  can  be  expressed  as  an  index  of  cultural  literacy for  the  use  of  curriculum  developers  and  educators.

claims.text: Very young children are naturally interested in extensive, limited information.
claims.source_passage: But very  young  children  are  interested  in  extensive,  limited  information.

claims.text: In the early grades, truly intensive study is difficult for students to pursue.
claims.source_passage: In  the  early  grades,  truly  intensive  study  is  in  any  case  difficult  to pursue.

claims.text: The extensive curriculum should be taught chiefly to young children because they enjoy picking up adult information before they can fully make sense of it.
claims.source_passage: The  predicted  difficulties  of  teaching  the  extensive  curric- ulum  simply  do  not  apply  to  young  children,  and  it's  chiefly  to young  children  that  the  extensive  curriculum  needs  to  be  taught. They  like  to  pick  up  adult  information  long  before  they  can  make sense  of  it.

claims.text: If students have not acquired the essential elements of the extensive curriculum by tenth grade, they will rarely be able to recover that knowledge gap.
claims.source_passage: A  special  motivation  for  this  focus  in  the  early  grades should  be  our  understanding  that  if  students  still  lack  important elements  of  the  extensive  curriculum  by  tenth  grade,  they  will  rarely be  able  to  make  up  the  loss.

claims.text: School readers should contain a larger proportion of nonfictional materials and traditional myths and stories than current curricula allow.
claims.source_passage: We  need  readers  that  contain  a much  larger  proportion  of  nonfictional  materials,  as  well  as  a  larger proportion  of  traditional  myths  and  stories.

claims.text: Textbooks should be prepared to systematically convey as much of the national vocabulary as possible.
claims.source_passage: Textbooks should be prepared so that they systematically convey as much of the national vocabulary as possible.

claims.text: Children are not as intrigued by imaginative literature as modern curriculum makers assume.
claims.source_passage: Children may not be as intrigued by "imaginative literature" as recent curriculum makers assume.

claims.text: Young children are fascinated by straightforward information and absorb it without strain.
claims.source_passage: Young children are fascinated by straight-forward information and absorb it without strain.

claims.text: Untraditional schools have failed to find an alternative to rote learning for basic subjects like the alphabet or multiplication tables.
claims.source_passage: Even untraditional schools have not found a way to avoid rote learning of the alphabet or the multiplication table or the months of the year.

claims.text: Children naturally memorize facts regardless of whether they are forced to do so.
claims.source_passage: Children don't have to be forced to memorize facts; they do it anyway.

claims.text: The failure of developmental curricula is that they cause children to memorize transitory materials rather than shared cultural knowledge.
claims.source_passage: The great oversight of a watered-down "developmental" curriculum for the early grades is that while our children are busily remembering whatever they experience in and out of school, their school materials are often transitory and unshared with others in the culture.

claims.text: A people is best unified by being taught their intellectual and moral heritage during childhood.
claims.source_passage: A people is best unified by being taught in childhood the best things in its intellectual and moral heritage.

claims.text: American cultural heritage is composite, drawing from Hebrew, Greek, Roman, English, French, and Teutonic elements.
claims.source_passage: Our own heritage is, like our ancestry, composite. Hebrew, Greek, Roman, English, French, and Teutonic elements are blended in our cultural past.

claims.text: Much of the content of cultural literacy is value-neutral and does not serve a specific moral or ideological agenda.
claims.source_passage: many early materials of cultural literacy do not attempt to foster "a common set of ideals" or to indoctrinate us in moral values.

claims.text: The national vocabulary frequently contains contradictory proverbs or 'dueling platitudes.'
claims.source_passage: Many a proverb has its opposite in the national vocabulary, so that often one discovers dueling platitudes.

claims.text: Schools should teach more survey courses that cover large movements of human thought and experience.
claims.source_passage: We should teach more surveys that cover large movements of human thought and experience.

claims.text: The 'critical thinking' movement is the latest version of educational formalism attacking the goal of teaching shared information.
claims.source_passage: Yet even now the goal of teaching shared information is under attack by the latest version of educational formalism, the "critical thinking" movement.

claims.text: The national vocabulary requires continued systematic attention within subject-based school teaching.
claims.source_passage: where teaching is done according to subject divisions, the national vocabulary needs continued systematic attention.

claims.text: Elements of cultural literacy should be incorporated into the structure of textbooks, courses, and school requirements.
claims.source_passage: We should incorporate the elements of cultural literacy into the structure of textbooks, courses, and school requirements.

claims.text: The denigration of 'mere facts' by proponents of critical thinking is a dangerous repetition of the educational mistakes made in 1918.
claims.source_passage: the denigration of "mere facts" by the movement's proponents is a dangerous repetition of the mistakes of 1918.

claims.text: Any educational movement that avoids specific content or fails to convey it to all citizens is committing a fundamental error.
claims.source_passage: Any educational movement that avoids coming to terms with the specific contents of literate education or evades the responsibility of conveying them to all citizens is committing a fundamental error.

claims.text: Deprecating facts as antiquated or irrelevant injures the cause of higher national literacy.
claims.source_passage: any movement that deprecates facts as antiquated or irrelevant injures the cause of higher national literacy.

claims.text: Young children store facts in their minds daily with astonishing voracity.
claims.source_passage: young children, who are storing facts in their minds every day with astonishing voracity.

claims.text: Instructional incoherence, rather than the teaching of facts, is what deadens the minds of children.
claims.source_passage: It is incoherence — our failure to ensure that a pattern of shared, vividly taught, and socially enabling knowledge will emerge from our instruction.

claims.text: The polarization of educationists into 'facts-people' versus 'skills-people' has no basis in reason.
claims.source_passage: The polarization of educationists into facts-people versus skills-people has no basis in reason.

claims.text: Facts and skills are inseparable.
claims.source_passage: Facts and skills are inseparable.

claims.text: There are no philosophical or practical barriers preventing the integration of higher-order skills instruction with traditional content.
claims.source_passage: No philosophical or practical barrier prevents them from doing so, and all who consider mature literacy to be a paramount aim of education will wish them to do so.

claims.text: The practical importance of ideas in human affairs is frequently forgotten despite being a well-established truth.
claims.source_passage: The practical importance of ideas in human affairs, while not a recent revelation, is one that is too easily forgotten.

claims.text: Education textbooks typically regard 'reading in the content areas' as inferior to reading modern fictional literature.
claims.source_passage: 'Reading in the content areas' is typically regarded as inferior to reading up-to-date fictions.

claims.text: The correlation between family background and achievement in the Coleman report reflects a truth about current public school practices rather than an inherent socio-economic inevitability.
claims.source_passage: All we can legitimately infer from the correlations between family background and academic achievement is that they reflect a truth about present-day public schools rather than a state of affairs inherent in the socioeconomic nature of things.

claims.text: The 1893 Committee of Ten's conception of geography was synonymous with modern ecology, integrating botany, zoology, astronomy, and meteorology.
claims.source_passage: For instance, it regarded geography as we do ecology, saying that it embraced 'not only a description of the surface of the earth, but also the elements of botany, zoology, astronomy and meteorology...'

claims.text: Educational half-truths are frequently more dangerous than outright mistakes because their plausibility allows them to endure.
claims.source_passage: But half-truths, because of their plausibility, are frequently more dangerous than downright mistakes.

claims.text: The dominant 'growth' conception in American education owes more to the romanticism of Jean-Jacques Rousseau than to the developmental psychology of Jean Piaget.
claims.source_passage: Although schools of education have clothed this romantic idea in the language of developmental psychology, their basic assumptions owe more to Rousseau than to Piaget.

claims.text: John Dewey was personally appalled by the 'neutral scientism' adopted by educational administrators who institutionalized his progressive ideas.
claims.source_passage: Dewey, in fact, became appalled by the neutral scientism of those American educational administrators who began to institutionalize progressive ideas.

claims.text: A child's temperament does not come pre-loaded with content; culture is something that must be acquired externally through learning.
claims.source_passage: The best teaching does accommodate itself to individual differences in temperament, but a child's temperament does not come freighted with content.

claims.text: The unconscious learning of transitory, unshared materials is the least useful form of memorization.
claims.source_passage: The unconscious learning of transitory materials is the least useful kind of memorization.

claims.text: The primary cause of student boredom and mental fatigue in school is instructional incoherence rather than the memorization of facts.
claims.source_passage: But it isn't facts that deaden the minds of young children... It is incoherence — our failure to ensure that a pattern of shared, vividly taught, and socially enabling knowledge will emerge from our instruction.

evidence.description: Standardized achievement test results showing black students score 35 to 45 percent lower than white students.
evidence.source_reference: William Raspberry, The Washington Post, September 16, 1985
evidence.source_passage: black students score 35 to 45 percent lower than white students in standardized achievement tests.

evidence.description: Report showing that disadvantaged children have equal technical reading skills at age six but diverge from peers later due to knowledge deficits.
evidence.source_reference: J. S. Chall, C. Snow, et al., Families and Literacy, 1982
evidence.source_passage: the technical reading skills of disadvantaged children at age six are still on a par with those of children from literate families. Yet only a year or so later, their reading skills begin to diverge according to socioeconomic status

evidence.description: A typical education textbook definition defining reading strictly as a recoding and decoding process.
evidence.source_reference: P. S. Anderson, Language Skills in Elementary Education, 1972
evidence.source_passage: Reading is a recoding and decoding process, in contrast with speaking and writing which involve encoding.

evidence.description: The Coleman Report of 1966, a study of 4,000 schools showing achievement correlates more with family background than school differences.
evidence.source_reference: James S. Coleman et al., Equality of Educational Opportunity (1966)
evidence.source_passage: This fatalistic conception was given currency by the famous Coleman report of 1966, which found, among other things, that differences in academic achievement were more strongly correlated with students' family backgrounds than with differences in the schools they attended.

evidence.description: Christopher Jencks's study 'Inequality' which supported the findings of the Coleman Report.
evidence.source_reference: C. Jencks et al., Inequality (1972)
evidence.source_passage: its conclusions were widely accepted and were later backed up by reports of the Carnegie Council on Children and a book-length study, Inequality, by Christopher Jencks and others.

evidence.description: Comparison between current language arts textbooks and textbooks from earlier decades regarding cultural information.
evidence.source_reference: Hirsch's own consultation of textbooks
evidence.source_passage: Their "developmental" approach contrasts sharply with textbooks from earlier decades, which consciously aimed to impart cultural literacy.

evidence.description: James S. Coleman's 1966 report on data from 4,000 schools showing correlation between background and achievement.
evidence.source_reference: James S. Coleman et al., Equality of Educational Opportunity (1966)

evidence.description: Asian-American students succeed despite lacking American background info due to existing traditions of literacy.
evidence.source_reference: Hirsch (authorial observation)

evidence.description: Walberg and Shanahan's analysis of a massive dataset identifying time-on-task as the key factor in achievement.
evidence.source_reference: H. J. Walberg and T. Shanahan, 'High School Effects on Individual Students' (1983)

evidence.description: The 1981 Coleman report based on a survey of 60,000 students comparing public and private schools.
evidence.source_reference: James S. Coleman (1981)

evidence.description: The 'New Coleman Report' (James S. Coleman), which showed that private schools produce better academic results for all students regardless of family background.
evidence.source_reference: James S. Coleman et al., Equality of Educational Opportunity (1966) / Ravitch (1985)

evidence.description: Variations in 9th-grade social studies and history focus across different American schools, demonstrating lack of curricular standardization.
evidence.source_reference: Powell, Farrar, and Cohen, The Shopping Mall High School (1985)

evidence.description: The Report of the Committee of Ten on Secondary School Studies (1893), which recommended a shared humanistic curriculum for all students.
evidence.source_reference: National Education Association (1893)

evidence.description: James H. Baker's minority report to the 1893 Committee of Ten, arguing against the indifference of subject matter choice.
evidence.source_reference: Eliot, Report of the Committee of Ten (1893), 56-57
evidence.source_passage: But one shrewd member of the committee, James H. Baker, then president of the University of Colorado, sniffed out this hint of educational formalism and filed a minority report objecting to it.

evidence.description: The seven fundamental aims of education established in the 1918 Cardinal Principles report.
evidence.source_reference: Kingsley, ed., Cardinal Principles of Secondary Education (1918), 7
evidence.source_passage: Instead, it stressed the seven fundamental aims of education in a democracy: "1. Health. 2. Command of fundamental processes. 3. Worthy home membership. 4. Vocation. 5. Citizenship. 6. Worthy use of leisure. 7. Ethical character."

evidence.description: Edward Thorndike's research on the lack of automatic transfer of skills between different areas of study.
evidence.source_reference: Cremin, The Transformation of the School, 110-115
evidence.source_passage: By 1918 educational psychology, particularly the work of Edward Thorndike, had vindicated Baker's objection to the idea that skills in one area are automatically transferred to another.

evidence.description: The 1918 report Cardinal Principles of Secondary Education, which advocated for diversified curricula and student-centered schooling.
evidence.source_reference: C. D. Kingsley, ed., Cardinal Principles of Secondary Education (1918)
evidence.source_passage: The Cardinal Principles therefore recommended introducing a wide array of courses that would accord with children's 'individual differences in capacities and aptitudes.'

evidence.description: Edward Thorndike's research in educational psychology which challenged the theory of formal discipline and mental transfer.
evidence.source_reference: Edward Thorndike (cited in Cremin, The Transformation of the School)
evidence.source_passage: By 1918 educational psychology, particularly the work of Edward Thorndike, had vindicated Baker's objection to the idea that skills in one area are automatically transferred to another.

evidence.description: Historical demographic data showing the doubling of the school population every decade leading up to 1918.
evidence.source_reference: Hirsch, page 121 (referencing school population growth)
evidence.source_passage: The later report tried to adjust school aims to the new situation created by immigration and a consistent doubling of the school population every ten years.

evidence.description: Children's use of the word 'mouses' as evidence of linguistic transfer/generalization.
Thorndike's own acknowledgement that a limited degree of transfer occurs.
evidence.source_reference: Cremin, The Transformation of the School, 113-14

evidence.description: The 1918 report's definition of 'fundamental processes' as reading, writing, and arithmetic.
evidence.source_reference: Kingsley, Cardinal Principles, 7-12

evidence.description: The concept of the 'shopping mall high school' and its horizontal fragmentation.
evidence.source_reference: Powell, Farrar, and Cohen, The Shopping Mall High School (1985)

evidence.description: Dewey's later writings expressing appallment at the neutral scientism of educational administrators.
evidence.source_reference: John Dewey, Experience and Education (1938)

evidence.description: A 1947 national conference ratification of a resolution asserting that 60 percent of youth required 'life adjustment training.'
Professor Diane Ravitch's summary of how the Life Adjustment conference characterized 'educationally neglected youth.'
evidence.source_reference: Ravitch, The Troubled Crusade, 65

evidence.description: The current practice of granting academic credit for courses like 'apartment and income property management.'
Charles H. Rathbone's assertion that no indispensable body of knowledge exists that every child should know.
evidence.source_reference: Charles Rathbone, 'The Implicit Rationale of the Open Education Classroom' (1971)

evidence.description: Roland S. Barth's writing in 1970 stating that children's own experiences are the subject matter of learning.
evidence.source_reference: Roland Barth, 'Teaching: The Way It Is /The Way It Could Be' (1970)

evidence.description: The 1980s counterreform movement, characterized as a grassroots movement by the American people returning to traditional curriculum.
Rapidly changing technology making narrow vocational skills obsolete.
Observation of literacy and conventionality levels in Americans in their teens and twenties raised under progressive education.
Patricia Graham's proposal arguing for both commonality and flexibility in American education.
evidence.source_reference: Patricia A. Graham, 'Schools: Cacophony About Practice, Silence About Purpose,' Daedalus 113 (Fall 1984)

evidence.description: Observation of limited shared associations regarding common terms like 'canaries' and 'Falstaff' among literate adults.
evidence.source_reference: Hirsch (authorial observation)
evidence.source_passage: People reliably share just a few associations about canaries, such as yellow, sing, kept in cages, but not much more. Literate people know who Falstaff is, that he is fat, likes to eat and drink, but they can't reliably name the Shakespeare plays in which he appears.

evidence.description: The necessity of knowing who Eisenhower was to understand the text of his Farewell Address.
evidence.source_reference: Hirsch (authorial observation)
evidence.source_passage: To understand the full text of Dwight D. Eisenhower's Farewell Address and the historical circumstances that gave rise to it, they have to know who Eisenhower was and what a farewell address is in the American tradition.

evidence.description: John Dewey's pedagogical recommendation regarding deep engagement with concrete instances.
evidence.source_reference: John Dewey (cited contextually)
evidence.source_passage: It coincides with Dewey's recommendation that children should be deeply engaged with a small number of typical concrete instances.

evidence.description: The 'Modern Major-General' lyrics illustrating the possession of extensive but non-intensive information.
evidence.source_reference: W. S. Gilbert, 'Pirates of Penzance'
evidence.source_passage: I am the very model of a modern Major-General, I've information vegetable, animal, and mineral...

evidence.description: The Major-General character in Gilbert and Sullivan's 'Pirates of Penzance' who possesses wide factual knowledge but lacks tactical expertise.
evidence.source_reference: W. S. Gilbert, Plays and Poems of W. S. Gilbert (1932)
evidence.source_passage: I've  information  vegetable,  animal,  and  mineral, I  know  the  kings  of  England,  and  I  quote  the  fights  historical, From  Marathon  to  Waterloo,  in  order  categorical

evidence.description: Research on compensatory education showing the difficulty of making up for cultural deprivation after the tenth grade.
evidence.source_reference: B. Bloom, A. Davis, and R. Hess, Compensatory Education for Cultural Deprivation (1965)
evidence.source_passage: if  students  still  lack  important elements  of  the  extensive  curriculum  by  tenth  grade,  they  will  rarely be  able  to  make  up  the  loss.

evidence.description: A study showing that students who lack extensive curriculum knowledge by tenth grade rarely recover from the deficit.
evidence.source_reference: B. Bloom, A. Davis, and R. Hess, Compensatory Education for Cultural Deprivation (1965)
evidence.source_passage: if students still lack important elements of the extensive curriculum by tenth grade, they will rarely be able to make up the loss.

evidence.description: The historical example of Charles and Mary Lamb's works written to introduce young children to sophisticated shared content.
evidence.source_reference: Charles and Mary Lamb, Tales from Shakespeare and The Adventures of Ulysses (early 19th century)

evidence.description: A popular early 20th-century textbook series explicitly designed to teach shared cultural content.
evidence.source_reference: A. H. Thorndike and F. T. Baker, Everyday Classics (1917-1922)
evidence.source_passage: Among the most popular elementary schoolbooks in America in the early part of this century was the series Everyday Classics by A. H. Thorndike... self-consciously designed to teach cultural literacy to very young children.

evidence.description: Nursery rhymes like 'Baa, Baa, Black Sheep' and 'Humpty Dumpty' as examples of content that transmits cultural literacy without moral ideals.
evidence.source_reference: Traditional nursery rhymes

evidence.description: Technical principles like the angle of incidence in physics or Gresham's law in economics as examples of value-neutral cultural literacy.
evidence.source_reference: General scientific and economic principles

evidence.description: Pairs of opposing proverbs (e.g., 'Too many cooks' vs. 'Many hands') demonstrating that the national vocabulary is not a unified value system.
evidence.source_reference: English language proverbs

evidence.description: Historical reference to the 1918 'Cardinal Principles' report as a point of educational failure.
evidence.source_reference: Kingsley, Cardinal Principles of Secondary Education (1918)

evidence.description: Literary satire of 'mere facts' used as evidence of the long-standing prejudice against factual instruction.
evidence.source_reference: Charles Dickens, Hard Times

concepts.author_definition: An educational theory that emphasizes natural development and general, content-neutral skills over the systematic acquisition of shared national knowledge.
The view that literacy consists of a set of transferable techniques or 'language arts skills' that can be developed through practice regardless of the specific content used.
concepts.common_alternative: Literacy as a knowledge-dependent activity requiring specific background information.
concepts.source_passage: formalism conceives of literacy as a set of techniques that can be developed by proper coaching and practice.

concepts.author_definition: The possession of the specific background information that a text assumes its readers already know but does not explicitly explain.
An instructional approach where children choose their own reading materials to stimulate interest and work at their own pace to master decoding.
Technical metrics used to evaluate the difficulty of texts, often used by modern textbook publishers at the expense of cultural content.
A curriculum that lacks a coherent, shared core of academic knowledge, often containing many nontraditional or vocational electives.
concepts.source_passage: It has been silently assumed that our recently fragmented school curriculum is a permanent and necessary feature of our educational system.

concepts.author_definition: The specific body of background information and shared knowledge necessary for mature reading and effective communication.
concepts.source_passage: Common sense predicts that children from literate households can more readily gain the literate culture necessary for reading than those from illiterate households.

concepts.author_definition: A psychological state in minority or disadvantaged children who lack models of academic success, leading to lower achievement.
concepts.source_passage: subtle psychological influences that intensify this effect, such as the academic defeatism of minority children who lack models of academic achievement.

concepts.author_definition: Horizontal fragmentation refers to the lack of consistency across different subjects in the same grade level; vertical fragmentation refers to the lack of sequence or consistency within the same subject across different schools or grades.
The proportion of school time actually spent engaged with academic content courses rather than vocational or non-traditional activities.
A romantic concept derived from Rousseau suggesting children have an inborn tendency to follow their own proper development, making education a process of following the child's natural instincts.
An educational aim focused on producing productive and happy citizens through 'immediately useful' life-skills rather than traditional academic subject matter.
Psychological principles used by 1918 reformers to evaluate subject matter based on how students acquire and apply knowledge to life, rather than the logic of the discipline itself.
The traditional belief that studying specific difficult subjects (like Latin or Greek) strengthens the mind generally, a concept rejected by 1918 reformers based on Thorndike's work.

A concept used to justify tracking and varied curricula; Hirsch identifies a 'high-minded' romantic side (unique value of the soul) and a 'practical' side (belief that some students are naturally smarter/less capable than others).
Defined by the 1918 report as reading, writing, and arithmetical computations; Hirsch argues these were wrongly treated as formal, content-neutral skills.
The psychological claim that training in one area does not assist in another; used by progressives to justify specialized vocational training over a general curriculum.
The system of grouping students by 'diverse capacities' (bright, average, poor), which Hirsch characterizes as creating academic castes.
A school characterized by extreme horizontal fragmentation and a wide, unguided variety of course offerings.
A curriculum that replaced history, directed toward 'activities of life' rather than the logical demands of a scientific subject, leading to a lack of shared historical knowledge.
An educational movement focused on guidance, citizenship, home life, and work experience for the 'neglected' 60 percent of students, later applied as a universal principle.
A 1960s-70s educational movement that held there is no indispensable body of knowledge and that a child's own experience is the primary content of learning.
The part of the school curriculum consisting of traditional literate knowledge—the information, attitudes, and assumptions shared by literate Americans (cultural literacy).
A complementary part of the curriculum (to the extensive) that allows for varied, adaptive means and depth in specific materials.
Narrow vocational education adjusted to the immediate needs of the workforce or moment.
An approach to teaching cultural literacy that avoids rote memorization of lists, instead presenting terms as part of a connected network of meaning.
Mental models that allow an individual to understand how isolated facts fit together in a coherent way, gained through intensive study.
The common stock of words, symbols, and information that children should systematically acquire to become culturally literate citizens.
Educational content that is temporary, local, or unshared across the wider culture, which children memorize in place of more useful cultural knowledge in developmental curricula.
The 'latest version' of educational formalism that aims to encourage 'higher order' skills but often does so by denigrating the importance of factual knowledge.
The failure of a curriculum to ensure that a clear pattern of shared, enabling knowledge emerges from instruction.
Advanced intellectual skills intended to go beyond minimal basic skills, which Hirsch argues are inseparable from factual knowledge despite being treated as independent by reformers.
The objective, value-free administrative approach to education that institutionalized progressive ideas while stripping away their humanistic spirit.
The blend of Hebrew, Greek, Roman, English, French, and Teutonic elements that constitute the cultural past of the American people.
A historical framing used to describe the shift from the 1893 Committee of Ten (Eden/Pre-Fall) to the 1918 Cardinal Principles (The Fall).

cases.name: The Coleman Report (1966)
cases.description: A massive federal study led by James Coleman involving 4,000 schools that found student achievement was more tied to family background than school quality. Hirsch argues that while the data was objective, its interpretation led to educational fatalism.
key_passages: This fatalistic conception was given currency by the famous Coleman report of 1966, which found, among other things, that differences in academic achievement were more strongly correlated with students' family backgrounds than with differences in the schools they attended.

cases.name: The Private School Advantage (1981)
cases.description: An analysis showing that private schools in the US achieve higher academic outcomes for all student demographics, even when accounting for family background. This success is attributed to a more academic, traditional curriculum that offers fewer vocational options and more shared literate information.
key_passages: private schools produce better academic results for all students, even when family backgrounds are taken into account.
curricula of private schools impart more literate information than those of public schools. Private schools offer fewer nontraditional and vocational courses

cases.name: Private Schools vs. Public Schools Study
cases.description: A comparison based on the Coleman report showing that private schools achieve higher results with diverse populations by focusing on content-heavy academic curricula rather than vocational tracks.
key_passages: private schools produce better academic results for all students, even when family backgrounds are taken into account.
Private schools offer fewer nontraditional and vocational courses and give each student proportionally more academic courses.

cases.name: The Committee of Ten (1893)
cases.description: A historic report by the National Education Association that recommended a traditional, humanistic curriculum for all high school students, assuming a shared base of knowledge.
key_passages: The earlier report assumed that all students would take the same humanistic subjects
recommended a traditional humanistic curriculum

cases.name: Cardinal Principles of Secondary Education (1918)
cases.description: A report that marked a decisive shift in American education by rejecting content-centrism in favor of seven socially-oriented goals, effectively 'Americanizing' the romantic ideas of Rousseau and the pragmatism of Dewey.
key_passages: In deliberate contrast to the 1893 report, Cardinal Principles of Secondary Education explicitly rejected the earlier focus on subject matter.

cases.name: The Cardinal Principles (1918)
cases.description: A report that reorganized secondary education to focus on diverse needs and individual differences, effectively creating the 'shopping mall' high school and introducing formalism to justify content fragmentation.
key_passages: It is tempting to see the 1918 document as the moment of our educational Fall
the emphasis on diversity required an acceptance of educational formalism.

cases.name: The Life Adjustment Movement (1940s-1950s)
cases.description: A movement starting in Washington D.C. that argued 60% of students were being neglected by traditional vocational or college-prep tracks and required 'life adjustment training' in areas like home life and occupational adjustment.
key_passages: The life adjustment movement of the 1940s and 1950s continued the advance of the Cardinal Principles.
It was regarded as a universal principle, 'best suited to meet the needs of all American youth.'

cases.name: The Open Education Movement (1960s-1970s)
cases.description: A more 'insistently romantic' version of progressive education that emphasized the quality of student experiences over fixed curricula.
key_passages: The 'open education' of the 1960s and 1970s was more insistently romantic even than the romantic-pragmatic principles of 1918.
In a real sense children's own experiences are the subject matter — the content of their learning.

cases.name: 1980s Educational Counterreform
cases.description: A grassroots movement in the United States seeking a return to traditional curriculum, meeting resistance from the educational establishment trained in earlier theories.
key_passages: The counterreform of the 1980s seems bent upon a return to a more traditional curriculum.
But the movement is being greeted unenthusiastically by many educators.

cases.name: Nineteenth-century British literate schoolboys
cases.description: Hirsch uses this group as a historical benchmark for a population that shared a high degree of extensive information (kings, battles, science) as common intellectual baggage, enabling shared communication even if it didn't confer specialized expertise.
key_passages: These  "cheerful  facts"  are  all  the  Major-General  knows,  and  they were  part  of  the  intellectual  baggage  possessed  by  every  literate schoolboy  in  nineteenth-century  Britain.  Everyone  in  Gilbert's  au- dience knew  those  same  facts

cases.name: Everyday Classics
cases.description: A textbook series by A.H. Thorndike and F.T. Baker from the early 20th century. It was designed to unify the American people by teaching a common stock of knowledge and ideals derived from a composite cultural heritage (Hebrew, Greek, Roman, etc.).
key_passages: In an age when the need of socializing and unifying our people is keenly felt, the value of a common stock of knowledge, a common set of ideals is obvious.
A people is best unified by being taught in childhood the best things in its intellectual and moral heritage.

thinkers.name: William Raspberry
thinkers.key_idea: Children who start school already knowing what society deems important find it easier to learn more; knowledge begets knowledge.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Raspberry's observation to validate the idea that prior information is the engine of further learning and achievement.
key_passages: We know that Raspberry is right, not only about achievement tests but also about reading and writing 'skills.' 'The more you know, the more you can learn.'

thinkers.name: Jeanne Chall
thinkers.key_idea: The reading gap between disadvantaged and advantaged children opens up after the initial decoding stage due to differences in background knowledge.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Chall to provide empirical evidence that the achievement gap is not a gap in technical 'skill' at age six, but a gap in cultural knowledge that manifests as students attempt to read for meaning.
key_passages: As Dr. Jeanne Chall has pointed out, the technical reading skills of disadvantaged children at age six are still on a par with those of children from literate families.

thinkers.name: James S. Coleman
thinkers.key_idea: Family background is the primary determinant of educational achievement, outweighing school-based factors.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch acknowledges the report's thoroughness and the reality of the correlation, but critiques the 'fatalistic' conclusion that schools cannot bridge the gap.
key_passages: Because the report was thorough and objective, its conclusions were widely accepted

thinkers.name: P. S. Anderson
thinkers.key_idea: The self-selection method of reading instruction where children choose their own materials.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Anderson's work as a representative example of the 'formalistic approach' that neglects cultural content.
key_passages: just this formalistic approach is recommended by P. S. Anderson as the "self-selection" method of reading instruction

thinkers.name: Diane Ravitch
thinkers.key_idea: The historical observation that the Coleman report led policymakers to believe schools were ineffectual, making it difficult to argue for any specific curriculum.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch quotes Ravitch to describe the negative historical impact of misinterpreting the first Coleman report.
key_passages: Diane Ravitch, tracing the aftermath of the Coleman report, makes this trenchant observation

thinkers.name: Herbert Walberg and Timothy Shanahan
thinkers.key_idea: Academic achievement is primarily a function of time spent studying the material to be mastered.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses their data analysis to support his claim that curriculum and time-on-task matter more than sociological determinants.
key_passages: In the computation made by Herbert Walberg and Timothy Shanahan from a huge sampling of data...

thinkers.name: James H. Baker
thinkers.key_idea: The curriculum should not be chosen based on its ability to 'train the mind' (formalism) but on the inherent value of the content.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch highlights Baker's minority report from 1893 as a 'shrewd' early identification of the dangers of educational formalism.
key_passages: one shrewd member of the committee, James H. Baker... sniffed out this hint of educational formalism and filed a minority report objecting to it.

thinkers.name: Jean-Jacques Rousseau
thinkers.key_idea: Naturalism and the theory of natural human growth as presented in 'Emile.'
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch argues Rousseau is the true, under-credited source of the romantic 'half-truths' that dominate American educational theory, specifically the focus on natural development over content.
key_passages: As the originator of these ideas, John Dewey is usually given too much credit (or blame), and Jean Rousseau too little. In basic educational assumptions Dewey was a disciple of Rousseau.

thinkers.name: John Dewey
thinkers.key_idea: Pragmatic emphasis on social utility and rejection of 'bookish' education.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch acknowledges Dewey's influence but characterizes him as a 'disciple of Rousseau' who attacked literate culture as rote-learned and abstract.
key_passages: Dewey attacked the abstract, rote-learned material of literate culture, which he considered to be... 'a weight / Heavy as frost and deep almost as life.'

thinkers.name: Edward Thorndike
thinkers.key_idea: The rejection of the 'transfer of skills' theory in educational psychology.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch notes that Thorndike's empirical findings were correct (skills don't automatically transfer), but argues these findings were used by reformers to throw out the entire traditional curriculum.
key_passages: By 1918 educational psychology, particularly the work of Edward Thorndike, had vindicated Baker's objection to the idea that skills in one area are automatically transferred to another.

thinkers.name: Charles H. Rathbone
thinkers.key_idea: There is no indispensable body of knowledge that every single child should know.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Rathbone as an extreme example of the 'romantic formalism' that he argues has damaged American education.
key_passages: The earlier reformers did not go as far as the sixties progressivist Charles H. Rathbone...

thinkers.name: Roland S. Barth
thinkers.key_idea: The child's own experience is the subject matter and content of learning, and adults should not judge the significance of that experience.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Barth's quote to demonstrate the logic of 'Open Education' and its disregard for shared cultural content.
key_passages: It is for time and future experience to judge the significance of a student's experience, not for the adult to judge.

thinkers.name: Patricia Graham
thinkers.key_idea: The need for both commonality in goals and flexibility in practice within American schools.
thinkers.source_work: Schools: Cacophony About Practice, Silence About Purpose (1984)
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses her proposal as the structural foundation for his intensive/extensive curriculum model.
key_passages: The curriculum reform that I have in mind accords with an eloquent proposal for educational reform offered by Dr. Patricia Graham, dean of the Harvard Graduate School of Education.

thinkers.name: B. Bloom
thinkers.key_idea: Compensatory education for cultural deprivation suggests a critical window for acquiring cultural knowledge.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Bloom's research to justify the claim that missing cultural literacy by 10th grade is a permanent loss.
key_passages: A  special  motivation  for  this  focus  in  the  early  grades should  be  our  understanding  that  if  students  still  lack  important elements  of  the  extensive  curriculum  by  tenth  grade,  they  will  rarely be  able  to  make  up  the  loss.

thinkers.name: A. H. Thorndike and F. T. Baker
thinkers.key_idea: The value of teaching a 'common stock of knowledge' in childhood to unify and socialize a composite people.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch quotes them extensively to prove that the goal of teaching shared content was once an explicit and respected aim of American education before the rise of formalism.
key_passages: Thorndike and Baker... self-consciously designed to teach cultural literacy to very young children.
Baker and Thorndike's method still responds to our needs.

thinkers.name: Charles and Mary Lamb
thinkers.key_idea: Adapting classic literature (Shakespeare, Homer) for very young children.
thinkers.source_work: Tales from Shakespeare; The Adventures of Ulysses
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites them as historical precedent for the type of 'new' (but historically established) materials he proposes for elementary education.
key_passages: Charles and Mary Lamb produced Tales from Shakespeare and The Adventures of Ulysses for very young children, for just the purposes I envision.

thinkers.name: Charles Dickens
thinkers.key_idea: The character of Mr. Gradgrind, who satirized the teaching of mere facts.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch acknowledges Dickens's influence in satirizing factual education but argues that Dickens was targeting a symptom rather than the actual cause of mental dullness (which Hirsch claims is incoherence).
key_passages: ...but also Charles Dickens who, in the figure of Mr. Gradgrind in Hard Times, satirized the teaching of mere facts.

actors.name: Schools of Education
actors.context: Accused of promulgating the 'faulty theories' (romantic formalism) that Hirsch blames for literacy decline.

actors.name: Educational Policymakers
actors.context: Critiqued for accepting and implementing formalistic theories in school systems.

actors.name: Professors of Education
actors.context: Accused of accepting the 'fatalistic' view that schools cannot improve the achievement of children from deprived backgrounds.

actors.name: Carnegie Council on Children
actors.context: Backed the conclusions of the Coleman Report regarding the impact of family background.

actors.name: Carnegie Council on Children
actors.context: Supported the findings of the first Coleman report.

actors.name: Asian-Americans
actors.context: Used as an example of a group whose existing literacy traditions mitigate a lack of American background knowledge.

actors.name: Diane Ravitch
actors.context: Historian whose interpretations of the Coleman report and the history of American education (The Schools We Deserve) Hirsch relies upon.

actors.name: Walberg and Shanahan
actors.context: Researchers who identified 'time on task' as a critical variable in school success.

actors.name: National Education Association
actors.context: The organization responsible for the 1893 and 1918 reports that shaped American curriculum.

actors.name: National Education Association
actors.context: The body responsible for both the 1893 and 1918 reports that shaped American secondary education.

actors.name: Schools of Education
actors.context: Accused by Hirsch of clothing romantic naturalism in the 'language of developmental psychology.'

actors.name: William Wordsworth
actors.context: Romantic poet whose ideas on childhood provided the 'underlying spirit' for the 1918 educational shift.

actors.name: National Education Association
actors.context: The organization responsible for both the 1893 Committee of Ten and the 1918 Commission on the Reorganization of Secondary Education.

actors.name: National Education Association
actors.context: Organization responsible for both the 1893 and 1918 reports.

actors.name: Clarence D. Kingsley
actors.context: Leader of the progressive movement and editor of the Cardinal Principles report.

actors.name: William H. Kilpatrick
actors.context: Progressive leader cited alongside Dewey and Kingsley.

actors.name: National Education Association
actors.context: Organization associated with the 1893 and 1918 reports that shaped American high schools.

actors.name: U.S. Office of Education
actors.context: Produced the report 'Life Adjustment Education for Every Youth'.

actors.name: School administrators and senior professors
actors.context: Resisters of the 1980s curriculum reforms due to their training in the 'Cardinal Principles'.

actors.name: The American People
actors.context: The driving force behind the grassroots 'counterreform' of the 1980s.

actors.name: Patricia Graham
actors.context: Dean of the Harvard Graduate School of Education whose reform ideas Hirsch adopts.

actors.name: Major-General (Gilbert and Sullivan)
actors.context: Used as a literary example of someone with extensive but non-intensive knowledge.

actors.name: Curriculum Developers
actors.context: The intended users of a proposed index of cultural literacy.

actors.name: Gilbert and Sullivan
actors.context: Authors of the comic opera used to illustrate the difference between extensive information and intensive knowledge.

actors.name: Critical Thinking Movement
actors.context: The latest version of educational formalism that Hirsch claims denigrates factual knowledge in favor of higher-order skills.

actors.name: National Education Association
actors.context: Contextual mention of 1918 reforms (Cardinal Principles) that Hirsch links to the modern critical thinking movement.

actors.name: critical thinking movement
actors.context: Modern educational movement Hirsch critiques for denigrating factual knowledge.

actors.name: skills-people
actors.context: Educationists who believe skills can and should be taught independently of specific content.

actors.name: facts-people
actors.context: One side of a polarized debate that Hirsch seeks to reconcile with proponents of skills.

objections_raised.objection: The decline in American education is caused by social changes, such as the disorientation of the family or the impact of television.
objections_raised.response: While these factors play a role, the chief cause is the 'faulty theories' (formalism) taught in education schools and accepted by policymakers.

objections_raised.objection: Reading is primarily a recoding and decoding process (a formal skill).
objections_raised.response: This is an oversimplification; once a student moves beyond the alphabetic code, reading for meaning depends entirely on background knowledge.

objections_raised.objection: Schools are powerless to improve the academic achievement of children from educationally deprived backgrounds because family background is the dominant factor.
objections_raised.source: James S. Coleman / Christopher Jencks (interpreted by educational theorists)
objections_raised.response: Hirsch argues this is a 'fatalistic' and 'paradoxical' mistake; while the correlation between family and achievement is real, it doesn't mean cultural information is unteachable in schools.

objections_raised.objection: Schools alone cannot do much to offset cultural deprivations or family inadequacies.
objections_raised.source: Consensus among educators after the 1966 Coleman Report
objections_raised.response: The 1981 Coleman report and the success of private schools with low-income students prove that schools can have a large effect if they use a focused, information-rich curriculum.

objections_raised.objection: Schools that cannot afford to teach all recommended subjects can still achieve the same educational result by 'training the mind' with whatever subjects they do offer.
objections_raised.response: James H. Baker (and Hirsch) argue this is a 'formalist' fallacy; the specific choice of content matters and cannot be replaced by general 'mental training.'

objections_raised.objection: Traditional subjects like Latin and Greek are valuable because they provide 'general mental discipline' or 'train the mind.'
objections_raised.response: James Baker (and later Thorndike) argued that observation and memory cannot be conceived in the abstract; power comes through specific knowledge, and skills do not automatically transfer.

objections_raised.objection: The 1918 Cardinal Principles report was a total failure—the 'moment of our educational Fall.'
objections_raised.response: The report was complex; while it introduced harmful formalism, it was more democratic in scope than the 1893 report and addressed real demographic pressures that the earlier report ignored.

objections_raised.objection: Training does not transfer between domains (e.g., studying Latin does not help with English).
objections_raised.response: Transfer is not absolute; humans use analogy to generalize, and many skills (like Latin helping English) involve direct application rather than theoretical 'transfer.'

objections_raised.objection: A return to traditional education might lead to the unimaginative teaching of facts as ends in themselves.
objections_raised.response: Dry incompetence (traditionalism done poorly) is not the necessary alternative to 'lively ignorance' (progressive schooling); facts can be taught vividly and imaginatively.

objections_raised.objection: Traditional content reflects 'higher values' that are the special property of Western culture.
objections_raised.response: Hirsch concedes that higher values exist in all traditions (e.g., Confucius), but insists on traditionalism regarding content because it is the only path to mature literacy.

objections_raised.objection: A common national curriculum would require all children to study identical materials (a rigid core).
objections_raised.response: The two-part curriculum allows for shared 'extensive' knowledge while permitting students to study different materials in depth (e.g., studying different Shakespeare plays) in the 'intensive' curriculum.

objections_raised.objection: Extensive knowledge cannot be taught as a mere catalogue of information in school; it is acquired through years of communication and must be embedded in interesting, intensive material.
objections_raised.response: Young children are naturally interested in facts, pick up information before making sense of it, and struggle with intensive study anyway, making early childhood the ideal time for the extensive curriculum.

objections_raised.objection: Children should not be subjected to rote learning or forced memorization of facts.
objections_raised.response: Children memorize facts naturally regardless of school policy; the problem is that modern curricula cause them to memorize transitory rather than culturally shared information.

objections_raised.objection: Education should focus on 'higher order' skills and critical thinking rather than 'mere facts.'
objections_raised.response: Denigrating facts is a dangerous repetition of historical educational mistakes; facts are necessary for the development of any effective skills.

objections_raised.objection: Facts are 'mere' data points that are antiquated, irrelevant, and deaden the minds of young children.
objections_raised.source: Critical thinking movement / Dickens (Mr. Gradgrind) / Rousseau / Dewey
objections_raised.response: Children actually store facts with voracity; it is the incoherence of the curriculum, not facts themselves, that deadens the mind. Furthermore, facts and skills are inseparable.
